entry.title: Re: Screen Painter Blank in SAPMV45A screen
entry.content:
Hello 

A blank Screen Painter (screen 0101 in SAPMV45A) is often caused by SAP GUI rendering/control issues rather than a screen definition problem.
Reset SAP GUI settings and clear the SAP GUI cache.

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Adv_ICo Sales_Configuration : movement type determina...
entry.content:
• Revisit your Adv_ICo Sales configuration and ensure you are using the correct movement types (683, 107, 109).

  • Remove the invalid “Issuing Valuated Stock in Transit” input and let SAP’s standard SIT logic handle valuation.

  • If you still face issues, check SAP Note references for Advanced Intercompany Sales configuration, as this is a known restriction in S/4HANA Cloud.  Check this SAP Help document Sell from Stock with Valuated Stock in Transit

entry.title: Ensuring Quality in BADI Implementations with ABAP Unit
entry.content:
Introduction

In a previous article, I described how to identify and implement the BADI FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION to enforce business rules during Profit Center maintenance.

However, implementing a solution is only part of the job. Once the enhancement is in production, new requirements, bug fixes, and refactoring activities can easily introduce regressions if the validation logic is not properly tested.

This is where ABAP Unit becomes essential. By creating automated tests for enhancement implementations, we can verify business rules consistently and gain confidence when evolving the solution over time.

In this article, I will share a practical example of how I tested a BADI implementation using ABAP Unit based on a real-world validation scenario.

Why Testing a BAdI Matters

BADI implementations often contain critical business logic. In many projects, this logic evolves gradually as new requirements are added. Without automated tests, every change introduces a risk of unexpected side effects.

The validation implemented in FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION ensures that the Segment value remains consistent with the last digit of the Profit Center. Whenever this rule is violated, an error message must be generated and the maintenance process must be blocked.

The business rule is relatively simple:

Last DigitExpected Segment
0GERAL
1ATO
2NAO_ATO

Although the rule itself is straightforward, it is a perfect candidate for automated testing because it contains clearly defined inputs and expected outcomes.

Test Class Design

Before creating individual test cases, I structured the test class to maximize readability and reduce duplication. The objective was to make each test focus only on the business rule being verified.

The class uses standard setup and teardown methods to create and release the class under test. This guarantees that each test executes in isolation and avoids side effects from previous executions.

METHOD setup.
  CREATE OBJECT me->f_cut.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD teardown.
  FREE me->f_cut.
  REFRESH me->messages.
ENDMETHOD.

The BADI method expects a FIN_ENH_PROFIT_CENTER structure as input and returns validation messages through the CT_MESSAGES parameter. Since several test cases share the same execution flow, I introduced a helper method named ASSERT_NO_VALIDATION_ERROR.

This helper method prepares test data, executes the BADI, handles unexpected runtime exceptions, and verifies that no validation message was raised.

METHOD assert_no_validation_error.
    " Given
    DATA(profit_center_data) = VALUE fin_enh_profit_center( validityenddate	               = `19000101`
                                                            controllingareaforedit         = `SI01`
                                                            profitcenterforedit	           = profitcenter
                                                            validityenddateforedit         = `99991231`
                                                            validitystartdate	             = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentername               = `Z999999999`
                                                            profitctrresponsiblepersonname = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcentercreationdate       = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentercreatedbyuser	     = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcenterstandardhierarchy	 = `0101`
                                                            segment                        = segment ).

    " When
    TRY.
        me->f_cut->if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute( EXPORTING is_profit_center = profit_center_data
                                                      CHANGING  ct_messages      = me->messages ).
      CATCH cx_ble_runtime_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
        cl_abap_unit_assert=>fail( msg = lx_error->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

    " Then
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_initial( act = me->messages ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Using a helper method significantly reduced code duplication and allowed the individual test methods to remain concise and focused on their specific scenario.

Implementing the Test Scenario

To validate the implementation comprehensively, I created tests covering three different categories of behavior.

The first category verifies situations where the last digit of the Profit Center is not one of the supported values (0, 1, or 2). In these cases, the validation should simply be ignored.

METHOD ignore_when_last_digit_not_012.
  ...
ENDMETHOD.

The second category focuses on invalid combinations. In the following example, the Profit Center ends with 0, which means the expected Segment should be GERAL. Since the supplied value is ATO, the validation must generate an error message.

METHOD error_when_last_digit_is_0.
    " Given
    DATA(profit_center_data) = VALUE fin_enh_profit_center( validityenddate	               = `19000101`
                                                            controllingareaforedit         = `SI01`
                                                            profitcenterforedit	           = `Z999999990`
                                                            validityenddateforedit         = `99991231`
                                                            validitystartdate	             = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentername               = `Z999999999`
                                                            profitctrresponsiblepersonname = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcentercreationdate       = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentercreatedbyuser	     = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcenterstandardhierarchy	 = `0101`
                                                            segment                        = `ATO` ).

    " When
    TRY.
        me->f_cut->if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute( EXPORTING is_profit_center = profit_center_data
                                                      CHANGING  ct_messages      = me->messages ).
      CATCH cx_ble_runtime_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
        cl_abap_unit_assert=>fail( msg = lx_error->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

    " Then
    DATA(expected_messages) = VALUE fin_enh_pc_message_t( ( msgid = `ZFIN`
                                                            msgty = `E`
                                                            msgno = 001
                                                            msgv1 = `GERAL` ) ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( act = me->messages          " Data object with current value
                                        exp = expected_messages ).  " Data object with expected type
  ENDMETHOD.

Finally, positive test cases verify that valid combinations do not generate any validation messages.

METHOD validate_when_last_digit_is_1.

  me->assert_no_validation_error(
    profitcenter = 'Z999999991'
    segment      = 'ATO' ).

ENDMETHOD.
METHOD validate_when_last_digit_is_2.

  me->assert_no_validation_error(
    profitcenter = 'Z999999992'
    segment      = 'NAO_ATO' ).

ENDMETHOD.

Lessons Learned and Clean ABAP Considerations

One interesting aspect of this exercise is how testability influenced the overall design of the solution. The need to create clear and maintainable tests encouraged the use of small methods, descriptive names, and reusable helper methods.

The resulting test class follows several Clean ABAP principles:

  • Small and focused test methods
  • Meaningful and descriptive names
  • Helper methods to eliminate duplication
  • Explicit use of me-> for clarity
  • Minimal shared state between tests

Another takeaway is that even relatively small BADI implementations benefit from automated testing. The effort required to create the tests was modest, while the long-term benefits in maintainability and confidence are substantial.

Final Thoughts

Implementing a BAdI is only the first step. Ensuring that the implementation remains reliable over time is equally important.

ABAP Unit provided a simple and effective way to validate the business rules implemented in FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION while making future changes much safer.

If you are developing custom validations through enhancement spots or BAdIs, I strongly recommend investing a little extra time in automated tests—it pays off quickly.

👉I would be interested to hear how other teams are testing their BAdI implementations and validation frameworks.

entry.title: Re: How to reverse/cancel the reservation document in the...
entry.content:
In the subcontracting process, once you perform the ASSIGN step, SAP creates a reservation document to link components to the subcontracting purchase order. Reversing or “unassigning” that reservation is not straightforward because SAP treats the assignment as a consumption requirement.

Use transaction MB22 to delete or adjust the reservation line items. If the reservation has already been partially consumed, you may only be able to adjust the remaining quantity. If goods issue has already been posted against the reservation, reverse it with MBST or MIGO → Cancellation. Then adjust/delete the reservation in MB22.

entry.title: Re: Sale order number is not displaying in CM25
entry.content: The blank field in CM25 is not a layout issue but a data issue. CM25 displays capacity requirements from planned orders, production orders, or reservations. The sales order number is only populated if the requirement is directly linked to a sales order (make-to-order scenario). If the requirement comes from a planned order without sales order reference, or from a stock order (make-to-stock), the sales order field will remain blank. Adding the field in CY38 only makes the column visible; it does not guarantee that the field will be filled.

entry.title: Reducing Total Cost of Ownership While Accelerating the Future of Commerce
entry.content:
A New Chapter for SAP Commerce Cloud 

Commerce is entering a new era defined by AI assistants, agentic experiences, autonomous workflows, and composable architectures. At the same time, organizations are under pressure to reduce operating costs, simplify technology landscapes, and maximize return on existing investments. SAP Commerce Cloud is addressing both priorities simultaneously through a set of initiatives focused on reducing total cost of ownership (TCO) while creating a clear path to future innovation. 

Modernization Without Disruption 

Our strategy is simple: help customers modernize at their own pace. Rather than forcing large-scale transformations based on product deadlines, SAP is providing flexibility, support, and automation that allows organizations to align modernization efforts with business priorities, budgets, and resource availability. 

This balanced approach reduces risk while ensuring customers remain positioned to take advantage of future innovations in AI, autonomous commerce, and omnichannel experiences. 

Key Announcements Driving Customer Value 

In line with this strategy, we have recently made a couple of key announcements: 

  • Accelerator UI templates will continue to be supported without a newly announced de-release date, providing greater planning flexibility. 
  • AI-based migration tooling is being developed to simplify the transition from Accelerator-based storefronts to modern headless experiences. 
  • Investments in automated framework update tooling will reduce effort associated with Java, Spring, and frontend technology upgrades. 
  • New storefront hosting and composable architecture capabilities will increase agility and lower operational complexity. 
  • AI assistants, payment innovations, and ecosystem partnerships with Google, Vercel, Adyen, PayPal, and Checkout.com will help accelerate business outcomes. 

Headless Commerce: The Foundation for the Agentic Future 

The future of SAP Commerce Cloud is headless, composable, and API-first. Headless architecture enables organizations to deliver experiences across channels while providing the foundation required for AI assistants and autonomous agents. As commerce becomes increasingly driven by machine-to-machine interactions, APIs become essential for innovation. 

AI-Based Transformation 

A major component of SAP’s TCO reduction strategy is the introduction of AI-based migration tooling. Planned migration capabilities will analyze existing Accelerator implementations and help convert them into modern architectures, accelerating projects that historically required significant manual effort. This translates into faster modernization projects, lower implementation costs, and improved developer productivity. 

Framework modernization remains critical. Supported versions of foundational technologies help ensure security, compliance, stability, and performance. SAP is investing in automation and AI-driven update tooling to make staying current significantly easier, reducing the effort traditionally required for major framework upgrades.

Building the Foundation for Autonomous Unified Commerce 

These investments directly support SAP’s vision for Autonomous Unified Commerce. AI assistants for merchandising, shopping, and order management will connect customer experiences with operational reality. Combined with ecosystem partnerships and composable architecture options, customers gain a powerful platform ready for the next generation of digital commerce. 

What Customers and Partners Should Do Next 

Customers should assess their current architecture, plan a roadmap toward headless commerce, and prioritize required framework updates that need be done no later than August 31st, 2026. Partners play a critical role in helping organizations identify opportunities to reduce TCO, modernize incrementally, and accelerate time-to-value through best practices and implementation expertise. 

The Bottom Line 

SAP is making modernization easier, less risky, and more cost-effective. By extending flexibility around Accelerator storefronts, investing in AI-powered migration and update tooling, and building a modern composable platform for autonomous commerce, SAP Commerce Cloud is helping customers reduce total cost of ownership today while preparing for the innovations of tomorrow. 

You can learn more about this by watching the replay of the Prepare for Agentic & Autonomous Commerce and Reduce the TCO of SAP Commerce Cloud webcast. And you can find more information about the changes to the planned removal date for deprecated Accelerator UI templates and extensions here.

entry.title: Internal Orders Locking mechanism

Thanks

entry.title: Outbound Delivery Orders – Picking in Base Unit of Measure
entry.content:
Hello,

We are using the Outbound Delivery Orders API and have encountered a problem when the unit of measure used in the Outbound Delivery differs from the unit used during picking.

For example:

  • 1 PAL = 900 KG
  • 1 bag = 25 KG

The Outbound Delivery is created for 1 PAL, but the warehouse picking is performed in bags.

If 1 bag (25 KG) is picked, the system then shows an open quantity of 0.972 PAL.

The issue is that this does not exactly represent the remaining quantity. After picking 25 KG from 900 KG, the remaining quantity should be 875 KG, which corresponds to 0.972222222... PAL. In other words, the quantity displayed in PAL no longer matches the exact open quantity because of the rounding applied during the unit conversion.

As more partial picks are performed, the situation becomes increasingly difficult to interpret and manage.

My question is:

  • Is there a standard SAP configuration or API parameter that allows the Outbound Delivery Orders API to return quantities in the warehouse/base unit of measure (for example, KG) instead of the delivery unit of measure (for example, PAL)?

The goal would be to keep the Outbound Delivery in PAL for commercial/logistical purposes while ensuring that picking transactions and open quantities are handled in the base unit of measure, avoiding rounding and unit conversion issues.

Has anyone faced a similar scenario? What approach or solution did you use?

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Unwanted change log is showing in business Partner
entry.content:
The API doesn’t just update the custom field you’re targeting; it re‑writes the entire BP segment you pass in. Even if you don’t change certain fields, the system may still record them as “changed” because the API overwrote them with the same value. This leads to “unwanted” entries in the change history (BUT000, BUT020, etc.), even though the values are identical.

What you can do is Pass only the fields you want to change in the API call. Use the X indicators correctly (set only for fields you want updated). Compare current BP values with your input before calling the API. Only include fields where the value is different.

entry.title: Re: Querying SAP SuccessFactors in Natural Language with Claude Desktop and the SF-MCP Server
entry.content:
@sapsf_itsupportb22ec1 

I'll let you know as soon as OAuth 2.0 support is in so you can move off Basic Auth ahead of the deprecation.

On write operations: yes, this is doable, and you're right that you can extend sf-mcp yourselves. The read tools already wrap the SuccessFactors OData API; creating/updating employee data uses the same API via upsert on entities like PerPerson, EmpEmployment, EmpJob, and PerPersonal. 

Contribution steps:

1. Fork the repo
2. Create a feature branch: git checkout -b feature/my-feature
3. Run tests: uv run pytest tests/ -v
4. Run linting: uv run ruff check .
5. Commit your changes
6. Open a PR

Two things to keep in mind if you go that route:

- Effective-dated entities (e.g. EmpJob) require an effective start date on write.
- Writes respect RBP, so the technical user needs the right permissions, worth scoping tightly.

I'd strongly suggest testing against a non-prod instance first.

Happy to review a PR if that helps.

entry.title: Problems with reporting period of Vietnam Balance Sheet & Income Statement
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

I have a problem with the Run Statutory Report app, report entity VN_RPG_ENT, report name VN_FIN_STMNT.

All the config in SSCUI: 101071 is default from SAP, only the Financial Statement Versions are our own.

The fiscal year of my company is from 2025/08/01 - 2026/07/31, so I create a reporting task for the same period.

But after running the reporting task, I get a blank result.

If I change the reporting period to 2026/01/01 - 2026/12/31, then the report shows some number.

I investigate further to the "Analyze Data" button and found out that SAP is inputting wrong parameter on "From Period" and "To Period" (From Comparison Fiscal Period, To Comparison Fiscal Period too). It should be 001-012 because of my company fiscal year.

Can it be assumed that this is the limitation of default statutory report? Or is there any way to solve the problem?

Thank you for your reading time.

entry.title: Re: Activate Batch Management Indicator
entry.content:
Hello @Amani_Mirdhah 

You can't activate batch management for the material because you still have a sales order. Check the following notes for the prerequisites to (de)activate batch management:

  • 1873593 - Batch management flag is not possible to set / unset
  • 1521082 - M3530 Change Material : Batch / Non Batch Managed - SAP ERP & SAP S/4HANA

Fulfilling those requirements might be difficult (clearing all the stocks, closing all the documents). Therefore, SAP delivers two utility reports to manage batch (de)activation:

  • 533276 - Setting the batch management requirement
  • 533383 - Resetting the batch management requirement

The tools are sold separately. Contact sap_dmlt_gce@sap.com and send them the filled out form attached to the notes.

I had used the report to set batch management. It worked flawlessly and saved us a lot of time. It's definitely worth the money, if you have many materials to convert.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Additional production system functionality in ChaRM with SolMan 7.2
entry.content:
Hi Miguel, I am facing exactly the same issue and would like to add our findings to help narrow down the root cause.

Our Landscape: We have a standard DEV → QAS → PRD landscape managed via ChaRM (Continuous Cycle / Urgent Corrections). After PRD, we would set a Reference System (REF) that should receive the same transports sequentially, i.e. after a successful import into PRD: DEV → QAS → PRD → REF

What we have already configured:

• A delivery route (Belieferungsweg) from PRD → REF is defined in STMS

• The STMS configuration has been distributed to all systems

• The REF system is defined also as a Production system in the Solution Manager System Landscape (Solution Administration)

The Problem: Despite this configuration, the REF system is not recognized as a valid track system in the Continuous Cycle landscape. When trying to add it manually as an "additional production system" (as described in SAP Note 2882691), the system is added but flagged as "without transport connection" — making it impossible to actually transport to it via ChaRM. Our specific open questions, which align with your:

1. Is sequential delivery (PRD → REF, not parallel) supported at all within ChaRM cycles — and if so, what is the correct setup path?

2. Does the REF system require a specific role or additional flag beyond being set as "Production" in order to be recognized with a transport connection?

Regards,

Sergey

entry.title: Re: not able to issue material error showing batch stock...
entry.content:
Hello @Project_manager 

How did you manage to active batch management with existing stock? SAP standard doesn't allow that, see Activate Batch Management Indicator. You must have change batch management flag directly in the database, hence your problems.

I'd suggest to deactivate batch management, the same way you have activated it. Clear the stocks and activate batch management again the standard way.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Why did MIRO post imbalances to GL account, why didn'...
entry.content:
Why MIRO allowed posting is because of the following reasons:

1) Invoice checks are based on PO history
MIRO compares the invoice quantity/amount against the PO and GR/IR balances. Since the GR was posted for 4 units, the system sees 4 units available in GR/IR clearing. Even though the PO was only for 2 units, MIRO allows invoicing up to the GR quantity.

2) Tolerance settings
Variance checks (quantity/price) are controlled by tolerance keys in customizing (transaction OMR6). If the tolerance key for quantity variance (DQ) or small differences (BD) is not set to block, MIRO will post despite the mismatch. In your case, the tolerance setup allowed the invoice to pass.

3) Over‑delivery acceptance
The PO item may have allowed over‑delivery (check item category settings in PO). If over‑delivery is permitted, GR for 4 units is valid, and MIRO will accept invoice for 4 units.

entry.title: Context Gravity vs. Data Gravity: Enabling actionable AI in distributed landscapes with SAP BDC
entry.content:
In recent years, there has been a strong push towards distributed data landscapes — bringing compute closer to where data lives (data gravity), enabling more capable AI across more data sources. But this shift has in many cases also moved AI further from the context needed to act on what it finds. This blog series is about the opposite force: context gravity — the pull that business context exerts on AI output, drawing predictions toward the systems where they become actionable.

Data gravity is a volume argument: compute should run where the majority of data is, because moving large datasets is expensive and slow. And it is a valid argument. Context gravity on the other hand is a different type. It is not about the volume of the data — SAP master data is modest in size compared to sensor streams or transaction logs. It is about the complexity, dynamics, and relationships contained in this data. A contract table might have a few hundred rows. But those rows are connected to customer hierarchies, credit exposure, service organizations, approval workflows, and penalty structures that have been modelled, governed, and maintained over many years. That network of facts and information is what makes a prediction actionable — and it is next to impossible to be replicated by simply moving a file.

The following example from a recent customer engagement makes that concept more concrete.

A manufacturing company had moved from selling equipment to selling outcomes — guaranteed uptime, defined OEE targets, contractual SLA commitments. The engagement covered ten assets, seven active contracts — a total at-risk exposure of roughly €20,000/day (illustrative figure) across the portfolio. The IoT sensor data from the shop floor was stored in a data platform; contract terms, financial data, maintenance records as usual in SAP. A model that could predict machine failure had no way, on its own, to know which contract was at risk, what the penalty clause said, or whether the right technician was available. A machine with 1.1 days to failure means something very different if the contract expires in 91 days than if it expires in 2. That gap — between a technically correct prediction and a decision someone (a human or an AI agent) could act on — is what this series is about. It discusses how SAP Business Data Cloud(BDC) helps to provide the right context for AI to make it business relevant.

This series consists of several blog posts, which will be updated once published:

  1. Overview: Context Gravity vs. Data Gravity: Enabling actionable AI in distributed landscapes with SAP BDC <- This blog post
  2. The business case: Outcome-Based Manufacturing in Practice: Managing SLA Risk Across Industrial Assets with AI
  3. Implementation and solution architecture: Cross-Platform AI Pipelines in Practice: Combining IoT Data and SAP-RPT-1 for SLA Risk Scoring
  4. Closing the loop: Outcome-Based Manufacturing and SAP Joule: From SLA Breach Prediction to Autonomous Prevention

Ok, let's start reviewing the details.

Two systems, one problem

The data landscape of this manufacturing customer matched the standard enterprise split: IoT sensor and operational data (shop floor) in the customer's data platform (here Snowflake), contract terms and financial data in SAP (top floor). The ML problem required both, and we considered two ways to bring them together — the two failure modes that enterprise AI projects on SAP landscapes typically fall into.

The first option: move the SAP data into the customer's data platform and run the model there. This would have solved the compute problem — the warehouse has the tools and the scale. But it would have created a governance problem. SAP master data is authoritative, frequently updated, and subject to strict access controls. Copying it into a warehouse introduces staleness, duplication, and compliance exposure. The model would have ended up running against a copy of the truth, not the truth itself.

The second option: build the model close to the SAP data, using SAP's analytics capabilities. This would have kept the data in place and avoided the governance risk. But it would have created an integration challenge — the IoT signals the model also needed were already established in the customer's data platform, alongside the pipelines, tooling, and data engineering expertise built around them. Moving that workload would have introduced the same copy-and-ETL problem in reverse. The model would have ended up working with an incomplete or delayed picture of the shop floor.

Both options reflect the complexity of enterprise landscapes where data is distributed by design — different systems optimized for different purposes. The mechanism that resolved this for the project was SAP BDC Connect, which links SAP Business Data Cloud to external data platforms using zero-copy technology — without copying data, without ETL pipelines, and without synchronization schedules. It keeps each system handling what it does best. More details can be found in this blog post.

The sharing via SAP BDC Connect works in both directions. SAP‑managed data products — e.g. plant maintenance records, contract master data — can be selectively shared outward to the data platform so that models running there have the specific slices of business context they need as training and inference inputs. Predictions and derived data products — time‑to‑maintenance scores, risk classifications, anomaly signals — can be shared back into BDC, as custom data products, where they can be joined with the rest of the SAP context and the authoritative semantics stay in one place.

A data product shared outward carries its values. It does not carry what surrounds it in SAP — the customer hierarchy it belongs to, the credit exposure linked to that account, the service organization responsible for a breach, the approval chain a penalty triggers. Those relationships are not columns in a table. They are a system: connected, governed, and continuously updated and trying to replicate that system outward is, in practice, rebuilding SAP in a different system. Context gravity is the architectural argument that it is cheaper — and more correct — to bring predictions to where that system lives than to move the system to where the predictions run. Because the actual business relevance comes from the whole context, rather than the context slices, which are available to the external platforms for their workloads. 

So, the strategic value of this setup was not just technical convenience. It meant the choice of data platform became a reversible decision rather than a constraint — providing more flexibility than in architectures where the context is baked into whichever warehouse you happened to build in first.

From sensor to risk score

Here is how this works in practice.

The diagram shows six steps that make up the full data flow:

  1. SAP source systems — S/4HANA plant maintenance records, asset master data, contract and financial data — are available through SAP Business Data Cloud as data products.
  2. IoT sensors and devices on the shop floor stream readings into the customer's data platform — vibration, temperature, pressure, fault history.
  3. SAP-managed data products are shared outward to the data platform via BDC Connect. No copy is made; the data remains authoritative in SAP.
  4. ML Model #1 runs in the data platform against the combined dataset. It produces one prediction per asset: days until next required maintenance (time-to-maintenance, TTM).
  5. The TTM predictions are shared back into SAP Business Data Cloud via BDC Connect as a custom data product — a single float per asset, traveling in the opposite direction to step 3.
  6. ML Model #2 runs in BDC, joining the TTM prediction against eight further features from SAP and calls SAP-RPT-1 via the Generative AI Hub to produce a breach probability per contract.

Let's review the two models in more detail.

ML Model #1 — Time-to-Maintenance (TTM) ran in the customer's data platform. It ingested the IoT sensor features — vibration, temperature, pressure — alongside plant maintenance records shared via BDC Connect. A Snowpark ML RandomForestRegressor, deployed as a stored procedure, produced a single prediction per asset: days until next required maintenance. The output — a small JSON array of floats — was shared back to SAP BDC via the BDC Connect.

 But, as mentioned before, the TTM prediction alone was not useful. A days-until-maintenance figure provided value only after it was placed against the contract it affects. Four examples from the implementation worth mentioning:

  • A sensor reading becomes a contract risk when you know the SLA target, the penalty rate, and the remaining days on that specific contract. Without that context, a vibration of 8.29 g RMS is a maintenance signal. With it, it is a €236,000 liability on a named customer.
  • A maintenance prediction becomes a dispatch decision when you know which technician holds the relevant certification and whether they are already committed to another job. Without that context, you send the wrong person and discover the conflict on site.
  • A breach probability becomes a financial provision when you know the GL account, the cost centre, and the document type the finance team needs. Without that context, the provision does not get posted until quarter-end.
  • A component failure becomes a portfolio exposure when you know which other assets share that component under the same contract. Without that context, you fix one machine and miss the one next to it.

All this information cannot be inferred from the sensor data alone. It is located in S/4HANA plant maintenance structures, in contract and financial master data, in the HR structures that know which technician holds which certification, etc.

ML Model #2 — SLA Breach Probability runs against BDC. The TTM prediction arrives as an input feature and joins against eight further features: OEE, asset health score, open fault count, spare parts lead time, contract remaining days, target uptime, current uptime, production capacity utilization. This is where the context makes the difference — eight of the nine features come from business data, including both master data and transactional context.

The model was SAP-RPT-1, SAP's Relational Pretrained foundation model, called via the Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core. RPT-1 uses in-context learning: you provide labelled historical rows directly in the API payload and the model scores the query rows against them. More details can be found in the following blog post.

The two models ran in sequence — TTM first, because its output is an input feature to Model #2. The orchestration was done via an agent built with LangGraph, which enforces this dependency through graph structure. More details will be shown in the technical blog post from the series.

The differentiating point in this pipeline were the contextual features, which were available within SAP BDC and made the breach probability meaningful. Without the SAP data, Model #2 cannot distinguish between a machine with 1.1 days to maintenance on a contract with 91 days and €2,855/day exposure (illustrative figure), and the same machine on a contract with 2 days remaining and no penalty clause. That gap — between a number and an actionable signal — is what the architecture is designed to close.

The main learnings from this project, can be summarized along the following four dimensions:

  • Design for context gravity, not data gravity. The classical data gravity argument says compute should run where the data is because moving data is expensive. That is correct for sensor data and transaction logs. But SAP data is different — it is not primarily a compute substrate, it is a context substrate. The right principle is that predictions should travel to where the context is, not the other way around or into yet another outdated copy of the SAP model.
  • The data platform choice is reversible; the context architecture less so. Choosing one data platform over another is an infrastructure decision that can be revisited. Choosing how to model contract terms, component hierarchies, and business relationships is a strategic decision that compounds over time. The SAP-managed data products in BDC together with the semantic layer is where the long-term AI advantage comes from.
  • RPT-1 simplifies ML operations. The traditional ML pipeline for a classification problem like SLA breach prediction requires training data, a training run, a model artefact, a deployment, a retrain schedule, and version management. RPT-1 replaced all of this with labelled examples in a context window and a single API call. 
  • The richer the semantic layer, the smarter the agents. An agent working with flat feature tables can score a risk. An agent that can also traverse contract coverage, component dependencies, and resource availability across the same context layer can turn that score into a decision. 

What comes next

The architecture described so far can tell you which contracts are at risk, how severe the exposure is, and roughly when a breach becomes likely.

The next posts in this series cover the full picture — the dashboard and agent interfaces built on top of this context architecture, how SAP Joule can operate as an action layer that surfaces risk proactively, and how a Knowledge Graph co-located with the context adds a relational dimension to what the models answer quantitatively. The context gravity principle stays the same throughout: the architecture does not change, only the intelligence layer above it is extended.

Have you seen similar patterns in your day-to-day business? If you are building on a similar stack or running into the same architectural questions, I am happy to compare notes.

entry.title: What's New in SAP Business AI for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
The SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release marks another important step in SAP's journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise. As SAP Business AI continues to evolve, it's no longer just about automating individual tasks. Instead, AI is becoming a trusted collaborator, handling repetitive work, surfacing the right insights, and coordinating increasingly complex business processes.

Think of a modern production line. Every station has its own responsibility, but the real efficiency comes from how smoothly everything works together. SAP Business AI plays a similar role across the enterprise, helping coordinate tasks, surface the right information at the right time, and keep business processes moving without interrupting the people who drive them.

The 2608 release brings this vision to life through innovations across multiple dimensions of enterprise AI. In this blog, we'll explore a selection of highlights spanning Agentic AI, the latest Joule innovations, and embedded AI capabilities across SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. From intelligent finance agents and AI-powered workspaces to natural language experiences and embedded business process intelligence, these innovations are designed to make everyday work more intuitive, efficient, and context-aware.

Rather than introducing AI as a standalone capability, these innovations embed intelligence directly into the flow of work, helping finance teams accelerate period-end closing, simplifying compliance management, improving project visibility, and making interactions with SAP software feel more natural than ever before.

Take a look at the short video below to see how these AI-driven innovations are helping organizations work smarter today while bringing SAP's vision of the Autonomous Enterprise to life:

 

Agentic AI highlights

Finance

Receivables & Payables Clearing Agent (planned for 2608.2)

Matching open receivables and payables is often a repetitive and time-consuming task. Finance teams need to manually review invoices, identify matching items, and validate clearing proposals, making the process prone to delays and human error.

The new Receivables and Payables Clearing Agent uses SAP Business AI to analyze open items and generate balanced clearing proposals. The agent provides clear explanations for its matching recommendations, allowing accountants to quickly review, validate, and approve proposals before they are automatically posted as clearing documents.

By automating invoice matching and providing transparent AI recommendations, the Clearing Agent helps finance teams accelerate period-end closing, reduce manual effort, and improve operational efficiency—allowing accountants to spend less time on repetitive reconciliation tasks and more time on value-added financial analysis.

 

 

Journal entry agent (planned for 2608.3)

Creating journal entries is an essential part of every finance team's work, but preparing them from spreadsheet data often involves repetitive manual steps. Mapping fields, validating information, and creating journal entries can take valuable time, especially during busy period-end closing activities.

The new Journal Entry Agent makes this process faster and easier. Simply upload your spreadsheet through Joule, and let the agent analyze the data, map it to the required journal entry fields, and generate journal entry proposals automatically. From there, finance teams can review, adjust, and approve the proposals in the Manage Journal Entry Proposals app before posting them.

By reducing manual effort while keeping users in full control, the Journal Entry Agent helps improve accuracy, accelerate period-end closing, and free finance professionals to spend less time on routine data preparation and more time on delivering valuable financial insights.

 

 

Joule innovation highlights

Joule for Business: Moving to SAP-managed

Getting started with AI should be simple. Today, customers need to activate and configure Joule themselves by setting up the required services, identities, roles, and integrations. While this provides flexibility, it also adds complexity before users can start benefiting from SAP Business AI.

Now, Joule is transitioning from a customer-managed to a SAP-managed experience. Instead of handling the technical setup themselves, customers will simply access Joule through SAP for Me, while SAP takes care of the underlying configuration and lifecycle management. This approach also enables SAP to deliver new Joule innovations and enhancements more quickly, ensuring customers can benefit from the latest AI capabilities as they become available.

By removing setup complexity, SAP-managed Joule allows customers to focus on using AI rather than configuring it. For existing customers, a lightweight migration path is planned, with additional details and timelines to be shared in the future. Customers currently implementing Joule can continue with the existing customer-managed setup and seamlessly migrate when SAP-managed Joule becomes available.

 

 

 

Joule for Consultants

Expert Workspace

As organizations increasingly rely on AI for different business scenarios, keeping AI assistants organized and easy to manage becomes just as important as creating them. Without a structured workspace, it can be difficult to find the right AI expert or maintain specialized knowledge across projects and teams.

Expert Workspace in Joule introduces a more streamlined way to organize and manage AI Experts. Each Expert can be tailored with its own instructions and reference files, enabling more focused, context-aware conversations for specific business scenarios. New search, tagging, and filtering capabilities make it easy to quickly find the right Expert, while improved visibility into attachment storage helps users manage their growing AI workspaces more effectively.

By making AI Experts easier to organize, discover, and reuse, Expert Workspace helps users build specialized AI assistants for different workflows, improving productivity while creating a more scalable and efficient AI experience across the enterprise. 

 

 

Joule Work

As AI becomes part of everyday business, users need more than a chatbot, they need an intelligent workspace that brings together insights, automates routine work, and helps them take action. Switching between applications and manually coordinating tasks slows decision-making and reduces productivity.

With the release 2608, Joule Work introduces a dynamic AI workspace that goes beyond chat. Powered by user intent and business context, Joule Work surfaces the right insights, coordinates AI agents and assistants, and helps users complete business tasks from a single place. Whether asking questions in natural language, working in AI-generated Spaces, or accessing AI-powered workflows across desktop and mobile, users can move seamlessly from insight to action.

By bringing conversations, AI-powered workspaces, and intelligent automation together in one experience, Joule Work helps users make faster decisions, reduce context switching, and focus on the work that drives business value: another important step toward SAP's vision of the Autonomous Enterprise.

 

 

Embedded AI highlights

User Experience

AI-assisted easy fill of SAP Fiori elements-based applications

One of the highlights of this release is the general availability of AI-assisted easy fill.

Business users often receive information through emails, meeting notes, chat messages, or phone conversations. Traditionally, they then have to transfer this information manually into ERP transactions—finding the correct fields, switching between screens, and ensuring every value is entered correctly. AI-assisted easy fill removes much of this manual effort.

By simply describing a business update in natural language, SAP Business AI automatically interprets the information and populates the appropriate fields within the business object. Users can then review the suggested values before confirming them, ensuring both speed and accuracy.

This not only reduces repetitive data entry, but also helps improve data quality, lowers the learning curve for new users, and allows experienced users to complete everyday business processes significantly faster.

 

AI-assisted easy filter

We're also expanding natural language capabilities with AI-assisted easy filter. Filtering business data has traditionally required users to know exactly which filter fields exist and how they should be combined. Now, instead of manually building complex filter criteria, users can simply describe what they're looking for in plain language.

SAP Business AI understands the request and automatically translates it into the appropriate filters, including complex combinations such as amounts, currencies, dates, sorting, and grouping. Recent queries and favorite searches are also available, making frequently used searches even faster.

The result is a more intuitive search experience that helps both occasional and experienced users find business information with less effort and greater confidence.

 

Environment, Health, and Safety

AI-assisted compliance management

Managing compliance obligations often means reviewing lengthy regulations, permits, or corporate policies and manually translating them into operational tasks. It's a time-consuming process that can increase the risk of errors or overlooked obligations.

With the 2608 release, AI-assisted compliance management helps automate this work. Users can upload compliance documents as PDFs, and SAP Business AI automatically identifies relevant requirements, proposes structured compliance obligations, and recommends the corresponding operational tasks—such as periodic inspections, maintenance notifications, or reporting activities. A side-by-side view of the original document and the AI-generated suggestions allows users to quickly validate every recommendation while remaining in full control.

By reducing manual data entry and accelerating the setup of compliance obligations, AI-assisted compliance management helps organizations improve accuracy, reduce compliance risks, and stay audit-ready. Instead of spending time extracting information from lengthy documents, compliance professionals can focus on managing risks and ensuring regulatory compliance.

 

Professional Services

AI-assisted aging work in progress analytics and management

For project-based businesses, keeping track of aging work in progress (WIP) is essential for maintaining healthy cash flow. But identifying overdue WIP, understanding its root causes, and deciding what action to take often requires time-consuming analysis across multiple reports.

AI-assisted aging work in progress analytics and management helps project financial controllers gain these insights faster. Using Joule, users can analyze and visualize WIP aging across defined time buckets, while AI automatically generates executive summaries that highlight critical items and recommend the next steps. Intelligent drill-down navigation and automated billing notifications make it easier to investigate issues and accelerate resolution.

By transforming complex project data into actionable insights, this innovation helps finance and project teams reduce manual analysis, speed up billing cycles, lower cash flow risks, and spend more time making informed decisions instead of creating reports.

 

 

Summary 

🎦Register for our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 Early Release Series! 

Between July 13-17, we are hosting a series of 24 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release. On our landing page you can register for the entire series or pick the sessions most relevant to you. 

 

🔎Find all our assets  

The release 2608 showcases more than 130 innovation highlights across the portfolio. Check out our release document for all the details!

We at Cloud ERP and SCM Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: POD 2.0: Data Scan for Barcode-enabled Process Order Component Consumption
entry.content:
Has anyone managed to implement Data Scan for Barcode-enabled Component consumption for Process manufacturing within the POD 2.0 framework?

I tried setting this up using a POD 2.0 based on the Order POD template and New POD 2.0 Framework: Implementing Data Scan for ... - SAP Community as a reference, but the 'Assemble Components' services does not work for Process Orders, so had to use had to use the Process Order Goods Issue service. 

However, I have faced some issues as some of the inputs required by this service are not available in the POD context. 

  1. BOM structure, which is mandatory if the component being consumed is a BOM component, is not available
  2. Phase. The POD context exposes 'Operation Activity' but the value is always null

This is the data scanning configuration I used

And the POD context values available:

As I mentioned, 'Operation Activity' is available, but is always null.

Any help/guidance is welcome.

entry.title: Re: How to Prepare for a Practical Certification
entry.content:
Hi @boam1004,

To prepare for the SAP Master Data Governance certification, I recommend the following steps:

  1. Start by building your knowledge step by step using the curated learning resources. Please complete them in the recommended order:
    https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/exploring-sap-master-data-governance

  2. Once you have completed the courses, you can register for the certification. You can find more information here:
    https://www.sap.com/training-certification/sap-certification.html

I hope that will help you!

Best regards,
Philip

entry.title: Re: Screen Painter Blank in SAPMV45A screen
entry.content:
Good remark, but I don't know how "Reset SAP GUI settings and clear the SAP GUI cache" could solve this.

Instead, I guess that SAP GUI is corrupted and reinstallation could solve the bug.

@Tanuj_Patil: you may also switch to the "alphanumeric painter" via the ABAP Workbench settings from the menu, to verify quickly that SAPMV45A 0101 contains a layout. Or display the tab of elements to make sure they are present.

If the alphanumeric painter shows the screen layout, the installation of SAP GUI for Windows is corrupted, so reinstall the latest version.

entry.title: Bank Statement Processing – Documents Not Displayed in Posting Proposal for Uncleared Rows (CSV Impo
entry.content:
We are using the Bank Statement Processing functionality in SAP Business One to perform automatic bank reconciliation by importing bank statements in CSV format.

The bank statement is imported successfully without any errors. However, after the import, when we click Posting Proposal for Uncleared Rows, the corresponding SAP Business One documents (such as Incoming Payments, Outgoing Payments, or other relevant transactions) that should be available for reconciliation are not displayed.

As a result, we are unable to proceed with the automatic reconciliation process.

Could you please guide us on the following:

  • What are the prerequisites for documents to appear in the Posting Proposal window?
  • What matching criteria does SAP Business One use to identify and display documents for automatic reconciliation?
  • Are there any mandatory configuration settings or mapping requirements that need to be completed before using this functionality?
  • Are there any mandatory fields in the imported CSV file that are required for the posting proposal to identify the corresponding SAP Business One documents?

Please let us know if there are any additional settings or checks that we should perform to resolve this issue.

entry.title: What Disappears in 2H 2026: SAP SuccessFactors Basic Auth & Direct Corporate IdP Integration
entry.content:
The single sentence

With the 2H 2026 release, SAP deletes three login-side capabilities from SAP SuccessFactors — UI Basic Authentication, third-party corporate IdP direct integration, and Partial Organization SSO — plus support for multiple asserting parties wired directly to SuccessFactors (KBA 3472405) [1][2][3][4]. After that, there is no direct authentication into SAP SuccessFactors — every login must flow through SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service (IAS), either as the main IdP or as a proxy in front of your corporate IdP [1][12].

 

The Preview-first trap most plans miss

Preview data centers lose Basic Authentication and direct-IdP login on October 12, 2026, roughly a month before Production [12]. Preview/Test is normally where you rehearse the migration. So:

If you haven't migrated your Preview tenant to IAS by mid-October 2026, you can lose basic-auth access to your own test environment before you've finished testing the migration you were going to run in Production.

The practical sequencing consequence: treat Preview as its own deadline (October 12), not as a warm-up you can leave until October. Migrate and validate Preview well before mid-October, so Preview is already on IAS and available to test against while you prepare Production for November 13.

 

What is deleted with the 2H 2026 release

Four distinct things, all under this one release. Be precise about each: they fail differently and you check for them differently.

1. Basic Authentication for the UI

End users logging into SuccessFactors with a username and password held in SuccessFactors itself, the "company ID + username + password" login screen. This is the classic native login.

What breaks: anyone who authenticates this way can no longer log in. They must instead authenticate through IAS (either against the IAS password store, or forwarded by IAS to your corporate IdP) [1]. Note that two populations are already IAS-only ahead of everyone else: new hires (onboardees) and external learners can only log in through IAS [5]. So if onboarding or external learning is live, part of this is effectively already in force for you.

2. Third-party corporate IdP direct integration

Your corporate identity provider (Entra ID / Azure AD, Okta, Ping, OneLogin, Google Workspace) configured as a SAML asserting party directly against SuccessFactors, with no SAP identity layer in between.

The word doing all the work is direct. You are not losing your corporate IdP. You are losing the ability to point it straight at SuccessFactors "without SAP Identity Services as proxy" [1]. The trust that currently terminates at SF must instead terminate at IAS, which then fronts SF. Your users keep authenticating against Entra/Okta/Ping exactly as before: the direct SF-to-IdP trust is what's removed [1][2].

What breaks: the direct SAML trust stops working, and users routed through it can't authenticate, unless that same IdP has been re-established as a corporate IdP inside IAS first.

3. Support for multiple asserting parties (direct)

A specific case of #2 worth calling out because larger orgs hit it: if you currently run more than one corporate IdP as multiple SAML asserting parties directly against SuccessFactors (e.g., different regions or business units, with a chooser screen or default asserting party), support for multiple asserting parties is deprecated by this date [4]. The replacement is to configure those same multiple IdPs as multiple corporate identity providers within IAS, with IAS doing the routing [4].

What breaks: the multi-IdP selection at the SF layer goes away; if you haven't rebuilt that routing in IAS, users behind the secondary asserting parties lose their path in.

4. Partial Organization SSO

The side-by-side pattern where one group of users authenticates through a corporate IdP while another group still logs in with a SuccessFactors username and password. Partial Organization SSO reached end of maintenance May 16, 2025 and is deleted with the 2H 2026 release [3].

What breaks: the side-by-side mechanism at the SF layer is removed. The capability itself, routing different populations to different authenticators, is rebuilt inside IAS as conditional authentication (KBA 2954556) [3][13]. So this isn't "you lose partial SSO forever," it's "partial SSO moves into IAS and you have to configure it there."

What "deleted" concretely means

Not "deprecated but tolerated." Deleted. SAP's language is unambiguous: after the deprecation completes, customers can no longer access SAP SuccessFactors with basic authentication or third-party corporate IdP direct integration [1][12]. In practice:

  • Basic-auth users and direct-IdP users who have not moved to IAS cannot authenticate: this is a login outage for those populations, not a warning banner [1].
  • New hires (onboardees) and external learners are already IAS-only, so unmigrated onboarding is the population that breaks first, not last [5].
  • IAS itself remains the answer for everyone: password users authenticate against IAS, and corporate-IdP users are forwarded by IAS to their IdP [4]. Everything ends up behind IAS.

Why SAP is doing this

Worth a paragraph, because it frames the migration as more than compliance homework [12]:

  • Basic authentication is simply weaker. It offers less security than the methods common to modern cloud applications: there's no additional mechanism to verify the user, and passwords are routinely reused across accounts and apps, which raises the risk of compromise. Moving to IAS is what unlocks multi-factor and risk-based authentication for SuccessFactors [12].
  • One identity platform instead of many point integrations. Putting IAS in front (as main or proxy IdP) unifies the login experience across all SAP applications and lets SAP harmonize UI and API authentication on the same platform, which in turn makes authorization management cleaner. It also cuts maintenance, because IAS ships pre-built integration and provisioning to SuccessFactors and other SAP products, rather than you hand-maintaining a direct IdP-to-SF trust [12].

And the flip side, if you don't migrate [12]:

  1. You can't adopt current security and compliance baselines such as MFA for cloud applications.
  2. You're left on an old, unsupported authentication architecture that is itself slated for deprecation.
  3. After the deprecation completes, you lose access via basic auth or direct-IdP entirely.

What is NOT going away with the 2H 2026 release (the confusion-killers)

This is where most planning goes wrong. Several things sound like they belong to this release and don't. Pin these down so nobody on your team conflates them.

 

API Basic Authentication (the OData/SOAP auth method)November 12, 2027 (phased)A separate deprecation on the API side. End of maintenance was June 2, 2023; deletion is November 12, 2027, with Basic Auth off by default in 1H 2027 (opt-in for migration) and fully disabled in 2H 2027. Don't let this later date lull you: login is November 13, 2026, and the EC items below die even earlier [6].
Login using SuccessFactors employment usernameMay 15, 2026 (earlier)A narrower login variant, deprecated ahead of the main date. After it, users can still log in via IAS (login name + password) or via basic auth using account login name until the login deprecation; employment-username logins must switch to account login name [5].
SOAP-based Employee Central SFAPIs (except CompoundEmployee), plus Basic Auth for EC integration connectivity (core hybrid / payroll, ref. ECT-263763)November 20, 2026API/integration items that die a year before general API Basic Auth (Nov 2027). If you run SOAP SFAPIs or core-hybrid/payroll integrations, this earlier date governs them, not the 2027 one [8].
SSO signing certificateAlready expired June 2, 2025Not a future date at all: it's already gone. If you're still direct, this already bit you [9].
Report Center SFTP report deliveryNot affectedUses the platform's internal scheduling framework, authenticated separately. No change required, a common false alarm (KBA 3724884) [7].

The one-line takeaway: login and API are separate tracks with different dates. Login/UI Basic Auth lands with the 2H 2026 release (Preview October 12, Production November 13, 2026). General API Basic Auth runs to November 12, 2027 (off by default in 1H 2027, fully disabled in 2H 2027); but SOAP SFAPIs and EC integration connectivity die a year earlier, on November 20, 2026, so 2027 is not blanket breathing room [1][6][8][12].

How to tell if you're exposed

Concrete self-checks, not a project: do these first.

  • Basic Auth (UI): if the SuccessFactors default username/password login page appears and a login succeeds, you have basic-auth users. If users land on the IAS login page or are redirected to their corporate IdP, they're already behind IAS [3].
  • Direct corporate IdP: in Provisioning under Single Sign-On (SSO) Settings, check your SAML asserting parties. A corporate IdP configured as an enabled asserting party directly (with IAS not the enabled asserting party) is a direct integration that must move [4].
  • Multiple asserting parties: more than one enabled asserting party (or a user-facing "choose your IdP" screen) means you're relying on the multi-asserting-party support that's being removed [4].
  • Partial Organization SSO: same test as Basic Auth: if some users hit the default SF username/password page and log in successfully while others are redirected, Partial Org SSO is still enabled and you're exposed [3].

If every population already lands on IAS or a corporate IdP (never the native SF login page), you may be further along than you think: IAS has been auto-deployed for tenants created after December 9, 2022 (KBA 2791410) [12].

What to do about it (the short version)

The fix for all four items is the same destination: move authentication onto IAS. The process runs through the Upgrade Center: run "Initiate the SAP Cloud Identity Services Identity Authentication Service Integration," configure IAS/IPS and your corporate-IdP trust (and rebuild multi-IdP routing and conditional/partial authentication inside IAS via KBA 2954556), then run "Activate SuccessFactors Identity Authentication Service Integration" (KBA 2791410) [12][13]. Activation is a one-way door and forces a successful test first, so do it in Preview before Production [12].

The reason to start now, not in Q4: SAP is running the Initiate (and then Activate) tasks for tenants not yet on IAS, on a mass-enablement schedule between July and September 2026, in support of the 2H 2026 release [11]. That keeps your users' access continuous, but an automated Activate is an uncontrolled cutover: no testing window you designed, no conditional-auth rules you built, no dry run. Running Initiate yourself, ahead of the automation, is how you keep control of the configuration and give yourself runway to validate before the release [11]. IAS adoption is mandatory by November 2026 regardless (KBA 3097769) [10].

Layer the Preview-first trap on top of this: with Preview deletion on October 12, your practical window to have Preview migrated and testable is even shorter than the November headline suggests.

Resources & getting help

SAP has put real enablement behind this migration. Use it [12]:

  • Announcement blog: Deprecation of Basic Auth / 3rd Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SuccessFactors.
  • Migration community blog: Migration to SAP Cloud Identity Authentication (IAS/IPS), with resources and how-to videos.
  • Mass-enablement blog: Mass enablement of Initiate IAS Upgrade and Activate IAS tasks to enforce…, the authoritative source for the July–September 2026 automation [11].
  • Help Portal: Migration to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service, and the release-note page for this deprecation [2].
  • Step-by-Step Guide of SF to IAS Integration Process and Common Troubleshooting Tips: SAP Community (search the title; a practical walkthrough with troubleshooting).
  • SAP Discovery Center: Mission: Learn to integrate HCM with SAP Cloud Identity Services in SAP SuccessFactors and migrate to IAS/IPS (SAP has moved the IAS/IPS e-learning here).
  • IAS/IPS Open Office Hours: now held once monthly via the SAP Learning Hub (a valid S-user and one-time Enterprise Support Academy sign-up are required); registration is linked from the migration community blog above.
  • If something breaks during migration, open an incident under component LOD-SF-PLT-IAS (Identity Authentication Services with BizX) [1][12].
  • For onboarding-specific IAS issues, use LOD-SF-OBX-IAS;
  • For IAS product issues, BC-IAM-IDS;
  • For IPS configuration, BC-IAM-IPS.

References

Numbered in order of first appearance. Every SAP KBA below is hyperlinked to its public SAP support page. Verify live dates on the SAP Help Portal before finalizing any plan. SAP has revised adjacent deadlines before, and the two-wave release timing (Preview October 12 / Production November 13, 2026) is exactly the kind of thing worth re-checking against the live page.

[1] SAP KBA 3472405: Deprecation of Basic Authentication and Third-Party Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite. End of maintenance June 2, 2025; deletion with the 2H 2026 release; "no direct integration to SAP SuccessFactors after deprecation." Component LOD-SF-PLT-IAS.

[2] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Basic Authentication and Third-Party Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite (What's New / release information page).

[3] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Partial Organization SSO in SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite. End of maintenance May 16, 2025; deleted November 13, 2026 (body text contains a stray "November 15, 2026"); includes the default-login-page detection test.

[4] SAP: Setting Up SAP SuccessFactors with Identity Authentication (integration admin guide). Source for IAS as real vs proxy IdP, asserting-party selection behavior, and "support for multiple asserting parties will also be deprecated by November 2026."

[5] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of User Login Using Employment Username. Employment-username login deprecated by May 15, 2026; basic auth deprecated by November 13, 2026; new hires (onboardees) and external learners are IAS-only.

[6] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Basic Authentication for APIs (the API side; deletion revised to November 12, 2027). See the API companion guide.

[7] SAP KBA 3724884: Potential impacts of Basic Authentication Deprecation for Report Center SFTP schedules (SFTP report delivery not affected).

[8] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Employee Central SFAPIs Except CompoundEmployee (deletion November 20, 2026 as publicly confirmed).

[9] SAP Help Portal: Renewal of SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite Single Sign-On Certificate (certificate expired June 2, 2025).

[10] SAP KBA 3097769: IAS / IPS – Is IAS implementation mandatory for all SuccessFactors customers? (mandatory by November 2026).

[11] SAP Community (Product and Customer Updates): Mass enablement of Initiate IAS Upgrade and Activate IAS tasks to enforce…, plus SAP's direct customer notice. Source for the July–September 2026 mass-enablement window and the automatic Initiate/Activate behavior.

[12] SAP KBA 2791410: Integrating SuccessFactors with Identity Authentication (IAS) through the Upgrade Center, together with SAP's customer communication for this deprecation. Source for the Initiate → configure → Activate process, IAS auto-deployment for tenants after December 9, 2022, the two-wave deletion dates (Preview October 12 / Production November 13, 2026), SAP's rationale, and the resource list.

[13] SAP KBA 2954556: How to implement Partial SSO after Identity Authentication (IAS) upgrade on SuccessFactors (the conditional-authentication replacement for Partial Organization SSO).

entry.title: Re: Bank Statement Processing – Documents Not Displayed i...
entry.content:
I think you might be using BSP the wrong way. When you import bank statement, the idea is that you have open invoices for which you will generate payments and reconcile them.

So when you click on Posting Proposal, the system should match bank statement line to some open invoice. After that, you Finalize the bank statement, which will generate payment document with postings and reconciliation between invoice and payment.

So now you need to set bank statement matching criteria so that Posting Proposal can match statement line with open invoice (by using payment reference most commonly).

entry.title: Gerüchteküche - erste eigene Funktionen: Einheit 2 Lektion 2
entry.content:
An English version of this lesson is available at: Get Coding with Snap! - Building Up to AI

 

Videolektion "Gerüchteküche"

In dieser Lektion beschäftigst du dich mit Funktionen - Programmbausteine, die einen Rückgabewert haben. Während du wilde Gerüchte verbreitest, lernst du deine ersten eigenen Funktionen zu bauen. Zudem erfährst du mehr über Listen, eine Datenstruktur, die mehrere Elemente umfassen kann. 

Folge den Schritten aus dem Video in Snap!. Die Aufgaben bauen teilweise auf das Projekt aus dem Video auf. 

 

 

 

Du bist dran

  • Erstelle einen weiteren Reporter (Funktion), der deine Sätze noch “gerüchtiger” macht, und füge diesen Block ebenfalls in deinen gossip Reporter ein. Hier sind einige mögliche Beispiele: 
    • “Hey, I’ve got news! “
    • “Did you know: “
    • “I’ve heard a crazy story. “

 

  • Schreibe ein “Wahrsage”-Programm. Lasse Benutzer:innen eine Frage stellen, die dein Programm beantwortet, indem zufällig aus einer Auswahl von Antworten ausgewählt wird. 
    Wahrsager:innen geben meist keine klaren und direkten Antworten - füge deshalb vor allem vage Aussagen wie “Dir steht eine aufregende Zukunft bevor” in deine Auswahl an Antworten ein.

    Um Fragen an eine Benutzer:in zu stellen, kannst du diesen Block verwenden:

    Die Antwort ist in diesem Block gespeichert:

     

  • Hier findest du die Aufgaben auch zum Herunterladen:

    • als PDF:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/PdcAdpy?rc=10&doi=SAP1326699 
    • als editierbare docx Version:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/sibDEgo?rc=10&doi=SAP1326700 

    Lösungsvorschläge für Einheit 2 - Lektion 1 findest du in dieser Collection .

 

 

Zusammenfassung - das hast du diese Lektion gelernt

Bausteine dieser Lektion: 

Erzeugt eine Liste, in der Zahlen, Text oder auch weitere Listen, Blöcke oder Objekte abgelegt werden können. Die Anzahl der Elemente in der Liste kann durch Klicken auf die Pfeilspitzen an der rechten Seite erweitert oder verringert werden.  
Berichtet ein bestimmtes Element aus der Liste, die sich im rechten Eingabefeld befindet. Das ausgegebene Element basiert auf der Eingabe im ersten Eingabefeld. Dort können Zahlen verwendet oder spezielle Eingaben wie "zufällig" aus dem Dropdown-Menü ausgewählt werden.  
Verbindet Buchstaben oder Wörter zu einem längeren Text.
Achte darauf, dass du die Leerzeichen nicht vergisst, wenn du mehrere Wörter zu einem Satz verbinden möchtest. Durch Klicken auf die Pfeilspitzen kannst du die Anzahl der Eingaben erweitern oder verringern. 

Notwendig in benutzerdefinierten Berichtsblöcken (auch Reporter oder Funktionen genannt). Berichtet den Wert im Eingabefeld in einer kleinen Sprechblase am Block. 

Verwendet in der Blockdefinition eines Berichtsblocks, berichtet dieser Berichtsblock den Wert im Eingabefeld des berichte Blocks. 

Weitere Blöcke, die sich in der Definition nach diesem Block befinden, werden nicht evaluiert. 

Kann mit dem Modul "Spracherkennung und Sprachausgabe" importiert werden. 
Benutzt die eingebaute Text-to-Speech Funktion des Browsers, um den Text im ersten Eingabefeld laut ausgeben zu lassen. Mit den anderen Eingaben können Akzent, Tonhöhe und Geschwindigkeit der Ausgabe angepasst werden. 

 

Listen

In dieser Lektion hast du dich mit Listen beschäftigt.

Listen sind sogenannte zusammengesetzte Datentypen. Das bedeutet, dass sie mehrere Werte zu einer einzigen Einheit zusammenfassen. Diese Werte können Zahlen, Texte, andere Listen oder sogar Objekte bzw. Blöcke sein.
Du hast bereits in der letzten Lektion eine Liste von Objekten verwendet, als du ein zufälliges Element aus Attribut andere Objekte ausgewählt hast – also ein zufälliges Objekt aus einer Liste der anderen Objekte im Projekt.

Listen sind geordnet. Das bedeutet, dass die Reihenfolge der Elemente erhalten bleibt und jedes Element einer Liste einen Index besitzt, über den darauf zugegriffen werden kann. Du kannst deshalb den Element  _ von _ Block benutzen, um auf bestimmte Elemente zuzugreifen. 

Die Werte in Listen müssen nicht eindeutig sein, derselbe Wert kann mehrfach in einer Liste vorkommen.

Neben Blöcken mit denen du auf Werte in einer Liste zugreifen kannst, gibt es auch Blöcke um neue Werte hinzufügen oder vorhandene Werte löschen. Alle roten Listenblöcke findest du in der Kategorie „Variablen“.

Die roten Listenblöcke findest du in der Kategorie „Variablen“. Listen werden mit dem Block Liste erstellt. Durch Klicken auf den nach rechts zeigenden Pfeil kannst du der Liste ein neues Element hinzufügen. Mit Umschalt-Klick auf den Pfeil werden gleich drei neue Elemente hinzugefügt. Ein Klick auf den nach links zeigenden Pfeil entfernt Elemente wieder. Listen können verschiedene Arten von Werten enthalten, zum Beispiel Zahlen, Texte, andere Listen, Objekte oder Blöcke.

 

Funktionen

In dieser Lektion hast du außerdem gelernt, wie du eine eigene Funktion (auch Reporter- oder Berichtsblock genannt) erstellen kannst – einen ovalen Block, der einen Wert zurückgibt.

In der Mathematik ist eine Funktion eine Vorschrift, die 0 oder mehr Eingaben entgegennimmt und daraus genau eine Ausgabe erzeugt. Dieselbe Eingabe liefert dabei immer dieselbe Ausgabe.

In der Informatik wird der Begriff Funktion etwas allgemeiner verwendet. Eine Funktion ist ein Teil einer Programmiersprache (in Snap! ein Block), der eine bestimmte Aufgabe ausführt und einen Wert zurückgibt. Funktionen sollten wenn möglich keinen Zustand speichern und keine Seiteneffekte haben.

Wenn du mehr über Funktionen erfahren möchtest, schau dir diese Vorlesung  (Englisch) aus „the Beauty and Joy of Computing“ von Dan Garcia an.

Um eine eigene Funktion zu erstellen, wählst du im Dialog „Neuer Block“ als Blocktyp „Funktion“ aus. Der „Block-Editor“ fügt der Blockdefinition dann automatisch einen berichte Block hinzu. Der Wert, den du in den Eingabeschlitz des berichte-Blocks einsetzt, wird beim Auswerten des Blocks in einer Sprechblase angezeigt.

Um eine benutzerdefinierte Funktion zu erstellen, wähle “Funktion” als Blockform aus. Dann füge das, was der Block berichten soll, in der Blockdefinition in das Eingabefeld des berichte-Blocks ein.

 

Module mit zusätzlichen Blöcken laden

Außerdem hast du gelernt, wie du thematische Module mit zusätzlichen Blöcken importieren kannst.

Gehe zum Datei-Menü und wähle „Module…“, um ein Dialogfenster mit allen Block-Erweiterungen zu öffnen. Wähle das gewünschte Modul aus und klicke auf „Importieren“, um die Blöcke des entsprechenden Moduls am Ende der Blockpalette hinzuzufügen.

Im oberen rechten Bereich des Dialogfensters befindet sich eine Vorschau, die alle Blöcke des ausgewählten Moduls anzeigt.

Klicke auf Datei-Menü – “Module…”, um ein Dialogfenster mit allen vorhandenen Modulen zu öffnen. Verwende die Suche, um auf ein bestimmtes Modul zuzugreifen oder scrolle durch alle Module. Du kannst die Blöcke des ausgewählten Moduls in einem Vorschaufenster auf der rechten Seite sehen. Klickst du auf “Importieren”, werden die Blöcke in dein Projekt geladen. Importierte Blöcke erscheinen wie benutzerdefinierte Blöcke am Ende der jeweiligen Kategorie.

 

 

Und jetzt?

Das wars mit Einheit 2 - Lektion 2: Gerüchteküche - erste eigene Funktionen.

  • Zur nächsten Lektion des Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/klonen-aus-eins-mach-viele-einheit-2-lektion-3/ba-p/14442210 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite von Einheit 2 geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/einheit-2-bausteine-f%C3%BCr-syst... 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite des gesamten Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/programmieren-mit-snap-building-u... 
  • Eine Englische Version des Kurses, bei der ein Zertifikat erworben werden kann, findet sich hier: https://learning.sap.com/courses/get-coding-with-snap-building-up-to-ai
